voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: Sahel region of Africa — arid grassland and savanna south of the Sahara
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its sense of smell guides it to dry grasses and fibrous plants.
Its eyes detect light level and movement across open, flat ground.
Its skin can absorb moisture from damp soil or air when water is available.
It feels vibrations through the ground from other tortoises and approaching animals.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in the dry grasslands and savanna of the Sahel belt in Africa.
It digs deep burrows to escape midday heat and stay cool underground.
Dry, tough grasses and fibrous plants are its primary food source.
Its home habitat can experience years with very little rainfall.
How does it send messages?: Males produce a loud, resonant call during the mating season.
Males ram each other with their shells to compete for the right to mate.
Shell color and size signal age and condition to nearby tortoises.
They move toward water sources after rain — a behavior linked to breeding activity.
What does it do when something changes?: During peak heat, it retreats into its burrow and stays underground.
In very dry periods it reduces activity and movement to save energy.
It is most active in early morning and late afternoon when cooler.
When approached, it pulls its head and legs inside its shell.
How does it learn?: Young tortoises hatch and must find food and shelter with no parental guidance.
They learn through direct experience which plants are available in their territory.
As they grow, they develop increasingly efficient foraging routes in their home range.
How does it have babies?: A female digs a ground nest and lays up to thirty eggs in it.
Eggs incubate in the warm soil for about ninety days before hatching.
Hatchlings dig out and are fully independent from the moment they hatch.
Young tortoises have softer shells that harden and thicken over several years.
What are its limits?: Cold temperatures are dangerous to it; it is built for heat only.
It needs dry, fibrous grasses to keep its digestion healthy.
Without burrows or shade, overheating is a serious risk.
Habitat loss and pet trade collection have made it Endangered.
What does this teach us?: Slow metabolism, dry diet, and deep burrows form one survival system.
Its shell grows and hardens over decades — a record on its back.
Every movement is timed around heat, food access, and safety.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Digs deep burrows that are essential shelter from Sahel-region midday heat.
Diet is based on dry, fibrous grasses; soft, watery produce causes problems.
Extremely long-lived, with shell growth tracking decades of its life.
Cold is a genuine health risk — built for heat, not cold tolerance.
still_debated: How far an individual's home range actually extends over multi-year periods.
The relative weight of ram-combat vs. scent signaling in male-male interactions.
How drought years shape long-term reproductive output.
common_failure_modes: Feeding watery vegetables as if they were safe staples.
Assuming all tortoises share the same temperature needs.
Underestimating adult size when planning for long-term care.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not treat it as a small or short-lived pet species.
Do not offer lettuce, cucumber, or fruit as staples.
Do not expose to sustained cold; burrow access or equivalent shelter is mandatory.
